title: 1 Year On, Cambodia Is Still Feeling the Impact of USAID’s Demise
gist: A year after USAID's abrupt shutdown, Cambodia is still absorbing the shock of a key donor pulling funding with no communication for months, including the cancellation of the five-year, $24 million climate-smart Morodok Baitang project.

insights.claim: USAID's Morodok Baitang, a climate-smart project meant to run five years with $24 million in funding, was among the many programs scrapped in Cambodia in 2025.
insights.speaker: Dana Zartner
insights.so_what: Quantifies the concrete development gap left by USAID's exit that other donors or Beijing may fill.
insights.locator: Local community members, private sector executives

insights.claim: US funding to Cambodia was halted five months before a US Embassy team could even meet the Cambodian government to discuss USAID's closure in late June 2025.
insights.speaker: Dana Zartner
insights.so_what: The communication vacuum, not just the money, damaged the bilateral relationship and donor credibility.
insights.locator: When a team from the U.S. Embassy

insights.claim: The abrupt halt of US development assistance to Cambodia with no communication for months was described as unprecedented by a former US in-country team member.
insights.speaker: Dana Zartner
insights.so_what: Signals reputational cost to the US as a reliable partner, opening space for alternative donors like China.
insights.locator: But the abrupt halt of development assistance

insights.claim: The loss of USAID funding hit Cambodia only two months into its fiscal year, making the timing particularly difficult for national development goals per a senior Cambodian official.
insights.speaker: Dana Zartner
insights.so_what: Mid-cycle donor exits create acute budget holes that recipient governments cannot quickly backfill.
insights.locator: According to one senior Cambodian official
